info.set_name: Comprehensive Neuro Set 2: Brain Stem, Cerebellum, Basal Ganglia (MCQ)

q1.prompt: Which neurotransmitter is released by the only output cells of the cerebellar cortex to provide inhibitory modulation of the deep cerebellar nuclei?

q2.prompt: Which brainstem structure is the primary source of the "climbing fibers" that provide powerful excitatory input to the dendrites of Purkinje cells?

q3.prompt: A localized lesion to which of the following structures is the most likely cause of hemiballismus, characterized by wild, flinging movements of the contralateral limbs?

q4.prompt: During histological examination of the cerebellar cortex, a researcher identifies axons of granule cells that bifurcate and synapse on the dendrites of many Purkinje cells. In which layer are these "parallel fibers" located?

q5.prompt: A descending motor tract originating in the brainstem is found to produce powerful stimulation of extensor muscles and inhibition of flexor muscles. This tract is the:

q6.prompt: In the basal ganglia circuitry, the activation of the "direct pathway" leads to which net physiological result?

options.label: Overall excitation of the motor cortex via disinhibition of the thalamus.
Overall inhibition of the motor cortex via increased drive to the subthalamic nucleus.
Decreased firing of the primary motor cortex due to increased GABA release from the striatum.
Direct activation of lower motoneurons in the anterior horn.
Inhibition of the lateral vestibulospinal tract to reduce extensor tone.

q7.prompt: A patient is administered a drug that acts as a potent D2 dopamine receptor antagonist in the striatum. What is the most likely effect on the activity of the motor cortex?

options.label: Increased excitation of the motor cortex due to disinhibition of the direct pathway.
Decreased activity of the subthalamic nucleus.
Net inhibition of the motor cortex due to increased activity of the indirect pathway.
Increased release of GABA from the external segment of the globus pallidus.
Direct stimulation of the rubrospinal tract flexor neurons.

q8.prompt: Which of the following best describes the physiological mechanism by which the cerebellum coordinates movement and minimizes "error"?

options.label: It generates the original motor blueprint and sends it to the primary motor cortex for execution.
It utilizes dopamine to balance the excitatory and inhibitory inputs within the striatum.
It provides direct monosynaptic excitation to alpha-motoneurons in the spinal cord.
It compares intended movements from the cortex with actual sensory feedback from the periphery.
It inhibits the medullary reticulospinal tract to maintain upright posture.

q9.prompt: An electrical recording from a single Purkinje cell shows high-frequency bursts of spikes, known as "complex spikes." These are characteristically produced by the activation of:

q10.prompt: A 70-year-old patient presents with a persistent "pill-rolling" tremor at rest and lead-pipe rigidity. These cardinal signs are most consistent with the degeneration of neurons in which structure?

q11.prompt: A 55-year-old patient exhibits a staggering gait and repeatedly overshoots the target during a finger-to-nose test. This lack of coordination (ataxia and dysmetria) indicates damage to which division?

options.label: Direct pathway of the basal ganglia.

q12.prompt: Following a midbrain stroke, a patient is observed to have "decerebrate rigidity" (rigid extension of all four limbs). This clinical presentation is best explained by the loss of inhibitory influences on which of the following?

options.label: Pontine reticular formation and lateral vestibular nucleus.

q13.prompt: A patient with Huntington's disease experiences choreic writhing movements due to the destruction of striatal GABAergic neurons. Theoretically, which change in basal ganglia outflow would be expected?

options.label: Increased inhibition of the thalamus by the internal globus pallidus.
Decreased drive to the motor cortex via the direct pathway.
Reduced firing rate of the subthalamic nucleus.
Increased excitatory drive to the motor cortex due to reduced inhibition.
Complete loss of dopaminergic input to the pars compacta.

q14.prompt: The pontine and medullary reticulospinal tracts have opposing effects on extensor muscle tone. A lesion that selectively eliminates the medullary tract while sparing the pontine tract would result in:

options.label: Increased flexor tone and intention tremor.
Loss of fine motor control in the fingers.
Inability to perform rapid alternating movements.

q15.prompt: In Parkinson's disease, the loss of dopaminergic input from the substantia nigra results in a shift in the balance of basal ganglia pathways. Which set of changes in the striatal pathways leads to increased inhibitory outflow from the internal globus pallidus?

options.label: Increased excitation of the direct pathway and increased inhibition of the indirect pathway.
Increased inhibition of the direct pathway and increased excitation of the indirect pathway.
Decreased inhibition of the direct pathway and increased inhibition of the indirect pathway.
Increased excitation of the direct pathway and decreased excitation of the indirect pathway.
Decreased excitation of the direct pathway and decreased inhibition of the indirect pathway.

q16.prompt: A patient presents with "intention tremor" and "dysdiadochokinesia." These symptoms are most likely caused by a disruption in the circuitry of which output system?

options.label: Subthalamic excitation of the globus pallidus.
Vestibular input to the flocculonodular lobe.
Inhibitory Purkinje cell modulation of deep cerebellar nuclei.
Alpha-gamma coactivation by the primary motor cortex.

q17.prompt: Which brainstem structure represents the anatomic crossing point for the descending pathways responsible for discrete voluntary movements of the contralateral hands and fingers?

q18.prompt: According to the "size principle" of motoneuron pools, why are smaller motoneurons recruited before larger ones during a voluntary movement?

options.label: Smaller motoneurons have larger cell membrane areas.
Smaller motoneurons have higher internal resistance.
Smaller motoneurons have lower thresholds for firing action potentials.
Smaller motoneurons are more sensitive to Renshaw cell inhibition.
Smaller motoneurons innervate fast-glycolytic (Type IIb) fibers.

q19.prompt: A patient presents with sudden, violent, uncontrollable movements of the left proximal limbs. A stroke is most likely located in which of the following?

q20.prompt: A neurophysiologist compares decorticate and decerebrate posturing. The arms are flexed in decorticate posturing but extended in decerebrate rigidity because:

options.label: The lateral vestibulospinal tract is lost in decorticate posturing.
The medullary reticulospinal tract is the primary flexor stimulus.
The red nucleus (rubrospinal tract) is spared in decorticate posturing.
The cerebellum provides intrinsic flexor drive to the spinal cord.
The primary motor cortex is inhibitory to all brainstem extensor centers.

q21.prompt: In the histological organization of the cerebellum, which layer contains the collections of synapses where mossy fibers meet the dendrites of granule cells?

q22.prompt: The primary function of the basal ganglia in motor control is to:

options.label: Directly stimulate lower motoneurons for the execution of reflex actions.
Coordinate the rate and force of ongoing movements via the flocculonodular lobe.
Modulate thalamic outflow to the motor cortex to aid in planning smooth movements.
Transmit conscious proprioception directly to the primary somatosensory cortex.
Acts as the primary relay for the corticospinal tract in the medullary pyramids.
